1: Human multiplication across the earth — the fruitfulness mandate of 1:28 operating, but in the context of the post-fall world. The growth of population is not itself evil, but it amplifies the reach of human sin. Luke 17:26–27 uses the days of Noah as a picture of the end times: “People were eating, drinking, marrying and being given in marriage up to the day Noah entered the ark. Then the flood came and destroyed them all.” The ordinary-life obliviousness before the flood is the template for the obliviousness before Christ’s return. As the flood came unexpectedly on a multiplying humanity, so Christ’s return will come on the world in its ordinary busyness.
2: The “sons of God” take the daughters of humans — whatever the exact identity of the bĕnê hāʾĕlōhîm (debated: divine beings, Sethites, or rulers/tyrants), the narrative describes a crossing of boundaries that God established and the further corruption of the created order. The episode illustrates the penetration of sin through every realm — human, social, possibly cosmic — that Christ’s redemption must address. Colossians 1:20 — “through him to reconcile to himself all things, whether things on earth or things in heaven” — is the scope of Christ’s reconciling work that matches the scope of the corruption here described.
3: “My Spirit will not contend with humans forever, for they are mortal.” God’s Spirit limits the time of grace before judgment. The same Spirit poured out at Pentecost without limit (Acts 2:17 — “I will pour out my Spirit on all people”) is the Spirit whose contending with humanity here has an expiration. Christ sends the Spirit to plead with the world (John 16:8 — “he will convict the world of guilt”) — but the contending has a limit. The Spirit’s withdrawal in judgment here anticipates the final sealing of judgment when Christ returns and the age of grace closes.
4: The Nephilim — the “fallen ones” or giants — and the “heroes of old, men of renown” represent the ultimate human arrogance: claiming divine-scale power and reputation in defiance of God. The pride of the Nephilim stands against the humility of Christ: “he did not consider equality with God something to be grasped, but made himself nothing” (Philippians 2:6–7). The men of renown who claim greatness through power are the anti-type of the servant-king who became great through self-emptying and obedience. Christ undoes the logic of the Nephilim by establishing a new and opposite definition of greatness (Matthew 20:26–28).
5: “Every inclination of the thoughts of the human heart was only evil all the time.” The diagnosis of total depravity before the flood is the same condition Paul diagnoses in Romans 3:10–18 — a catena of OT texts establishing that there is no one righteous, not even one. The flood is the OT’s answer to total corruption; the gospel is the NT’s answer. Christ comes to address the same condition that made the flood necessary: “What a wretched man I am! Who will rescue me from this body that is subject to death? Thanks be to God, who delivers me through Jesus Christ our Lord!” (Romans 7:24–25). The every-inclination-only-evil of v5 is the problem Christ’s death and the Spirit’s renewal address.
6: “The LORD regretted that he had made human beings on the earth, and his heart was deeply troubled.” The divine grief (nāḥam — the same root as Noah’s name) over sin’s devastation is the emotion that will find its fullest expression in the incarnate Son: “he was a man of sorrows and familiar with suffering” (Isaiah 53:3); “Jesus wept” (John 11:35) over sin’s consequences. The God who is grieved by human sin in v6 is the same God who enters human grief in Christ — not as a distant observer but as one who bears the grief himself. Divine sorrow over sin is not a contradiction of divine love but its deepest expression.
7: God’s declaration of judgment — “I will wipe from the face of the earth the human race I have created.” The flood is a type of the final judgment that Christ will execute. 2 Peter 3:5–7 makes the comparison explicit: “By these waters also the world of that time was deluged and destroyed. By the same word the present heavens and earth are reserved for fire, being kept for the day of judgment.” The flood judges the pre-Christ world; Christ will judge the world at his return. Acts 17:31 — “he has set a day when he will judge the world with justice by the man he has appointed.” The flood previews the final judgment; Christ executes both the rescue and the judgment it foreshadows.
8: “But Noah found favor (ḥēn) in the eyes of the LORD.” The first use of “grace” in the Bible. The ḥēn — undeserved favor — that rescues Noah from the flood is the principle that runs to its fullest expression in Ephesians 2:8–9: “it is by grace you have been saved, through faith — and this is not from yourselves, it is the gift of God.” As Noah did nothing to earn the flood’s escape but received divine favor, so believers are saved not by works but by grace through Christ. The “but Noah found favor” against the backdrop of universal human corruption is the structure of every gospel: unearned grace selecting one from among the condemned.
9: Noah was “righteous, blameless among the people of his time,” and “walked faithfully with God.” Three epithets that carry Christological freight. Righteous (ṣaddîq): Christ is the Righteous One (Acts 3:14; Genesis 7:52; 1 John 2:1) whose righteousness is credited to believers (Romans 4:5). Blameless (tāmîm): the same word used for the unblemished Passover lamb (Exodus 12:5); Christ is the “lamb without blemish or defect” (1 Peter 1:19). Walking with God: the restored communion that Christ brings (John 14:6 — “no one comes to the Father except through me”). Noah’s three epithets are a shadow of what Christ is in fullness and truth.
10: Noah’s three sons — Shem, Ham, and Japheth — are the post-flood source of all humanity. Genesis 10’s table of nations traces seventy nations from these three sons. Christ’s redemption gathers from every nation: “you purchased for God persons from every tribe and language and people and nation” (Revelation 5:9). The scope of Noah’s family as the origin of all nations is the scope of Christ’s redemptive reach. The seventy nations of Genesis 10 match the seventy disciples Jesus sends out in Luke 10:1 — the mission of Christ is explicitly to the scope of the nations descending from Noah’s sons.
11: “The earth was corrupt in God’s sight and was full of violence (ḥāmās).” The corruption and violence that fills the pre-flood earth is the condition into which God sends his Son — “he came to what was his own, but his own did not receive him” (John 1:11). Christ enters the violent, corrupt world not to condemn it (John 3:17) but to take its violence upon himself (Isaiah 53:9 — “though he had done no violence”, he bore violent death). The world of v11 is the world that the cross addresses: God reconciling to himself the violent and corrupt world he made.
12: “All the people on earth had corrupted their ways.” The universal scope of corruption — every person, every path distorted — is the diagnosis that makes the gospel necessary. Paul’s argument in Rom 1–3 moves through the same logic: all have sinned (Romans 3:23), none righteous, therefore the righteousness of God through faith in Christ is the only remedy. The flood is the OT response to universal corruption; the cross is the definitive response. Christ does not merely reset the world as the flood did; he provides the new creation that does not revert to the corruption the flood temporarily interrupted.
13: God speaks judgment and salvation to Noah simultaneously — “I am going to put an end to all people… So make yourself an ark.” The same divine word announces destruction and provides escape. This is the pattern of the gospel: “whoever does not believe stands condemned already” (John 3:18), but Christ is the provision of escape from the condemnation. God does not leave Noah without warning or provision; he does not leave the world without warning or provision either — Christ is the ark of refuge from the coming judgment (John 5:24 — “whoever hears my word and believes him who sent me has eternal life and will not be judged”).
14: The ark made of gōper-wood, coated inside and out with kōper (pitch or bitumen). The Hebrew word kōper shares its consonantal root with kipper (to cover/atone), the root of kappōret (the mercy seat) and Yom Kippur (Day of Atonement). The covering that protects Noah from the flood judgment is linguistically linked to the covering that protects Israel from divine wrath on the Day of Atonement — and both point toward Christ. 1 Peter 3:20–21 explicitly draws the ark-to-Christ line: “in the ark… only a few people were saved through water, and this water symbolizes baptism that now saves you also — by the resurrection of Jesus Christ.” The covering of the ark is the type of the atonement Christ provides.
15: The precise dimensions of the ark — 300 by 50 by 30 cubits — signal a real, constructed vessel, not a symbol. The ark’s dimensions have been compared to seaworthy vessels by marine architects — the ratio of length to breadth is within the range of modern ship design. The physicality of the ark matters: salvation through Christ is not mythological but historical. As the ark was a real vessel in which real people survived a real judgment, so Christ’s death was a real death in real history that accomplishes real atonement. The physical specificity of the ark corresponds to the physical specificity of the cross.
16: The ṣōhar — a roof opening or light-source — is built into the ark, along with a single door. The one door of the ark is a type: “I am the gate; whoever enters through me will be saved” (John 10:9). As there was one way into the ark — through the single door — there is one way to salvation through the flood of divine judgment: through Christ. Augustine made this connection; the NT logic of Christ as the exclusive way (John 14:6) gives it its warrant. The single door of the ark is the single door of the gospel.
17: God announces the flood — “everything on earth will perish.” The comprehensive scope of the judgment is what makes the ark’s rescue comprehensive for those inside. The logic of salvation through Christ follows the same pattern: the scope of death is the scope of Christ’s rescue — “as in Adam all die, so in Christ all will be made alive” (1 Corinthians 15:22). The universal death sentence of the flood is the type of the universal death sentence under sin that makes the universal offer of rescue through Christ both necessary and gracious.
18: “But I will establish my covenant with you.” The first use of bĕrît (covenant) in the Bible. This Noahic covenant is the first in the chain: Noahic → Abrahamic → Mosaic → Davidic → New Covenant. Each covenant in the chain escalates and clarifies what God is doing; the New Covenant through Christ is the fulfillment of all. Hebrews 12:24 — “Jesus the mediator of a new covenant” — stands at the end of the covenant chain that begins here. The God who makes covenants — binding himself by oath to preserve his people through judgment — is the God who sends his Son as the covenant guarantee (Hebrews 7:22 — “Jesus has become the guarantor of a better covenant”).
19: Two of every creature into the ark — preservation of the created order through the judgment. The scope of salvation is not merely human but creational: God preserves the creatures he made. The new creation that Christ brings is similarly creational in scope: “creation itself will be liberated from its bondage to decay and brought into the freedom and glory of the children of God” (Romans 8:21). The ark’s preservation of animals through the flood is the type of the new creation’s preservation and renewal of the whole created order through Christ’s redemption.
20: Every kind of bird and animal coming to the ark to be kept alive — the creatures responding to the divine summons. The gathering of all kinds of creatures around the saving vessel anticipates the eschatological gathering: “all nations will be gathered before him” (Matthew 25:32); “every tribe and language and people and nation” redeemed by Christ (Revelation 5:9). The ark that gathered every kind of living creature is the type of the gospel that gathers every kind of human being: “Go into all the world and preach the gospel to all creation” (Mark 16:15). The scope of the ark’s gathering is the scope of Christ’s mission.
21: God commands food to be stored in the ark — provision for the journey through judgment. The God who provides food in the ark is the God who provides manna in the wilderness and who, in Christ, declares himself “the bread of life” (John 6:35) — the provision that sustains his people through every trial. The ark is provisioned for survival; Christ’s people are provisioned with “every spiritual blessing in Christ” (Ephesians 1:3). The stored food of the ark points toward the spiritual sustenance Christ supplies to those who shelter in him through the judgment of sin and death.
22: “Noah did everything just as God commanded him.” The perfect obedience of Noah is a type of the perfect obedience of Christ. Hebrews 11:7 frames Noah’s obedience as faith: “by faith Noah, when warned about things not yet seen, in holy fear built an ark to save his family.” Christ’s obedience is greater: “he humbled himself by becoming obedient to death — even death on a cross” (Philippians 2:8). Noah obeyed God’s command to build; Christ obeyed the Father’s will to die. Romans 5:19 — “through the obedience of the one man the many will be made righteous” — is the ultimate statement of which Noah’s complete obedience is the shadow. Noah did everything God commanded; Christ did everything the Father required — and his obedience becomes the righteousness of all who are in him.
